coredata.dc:title: A molecularly imprinted polymers/carbon dots-grafted paper sensor for 3-monochloropropane-1,2-diol determination
coredata.dc:creator: Fang, Min
Zhou, Lv
Zhang, Hu
Liu, Liang
Gong, Zhi-Yong
coredata.dc:description: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-diol (3-MCPD) is a common food processing contaminant and a simple, rapid, sensitive and low cost monitoring technology is needed due to its potential carcinogenic nature. Carbon dots directly intercepted on filter paper provide high fluorescence intensity and can be adapted for use as a sensor. We synthesized a carbon dot-filter paper in combination with a molecularly imprinted polymeric film to extract 3-MCPD from samples. This grafted paper-based sensor exhibited a high adsorption capacity (68.97 mg g−1), an excellent selectivity (imprinting factor = 4.5) and a low detection limit (0.6 ng mL−1). Recoveries ranged from 97.2% to 105.3% with relative standard deviations <5.6%. The results obtained using this method were linearly correlated to those of the classic GC–MS method (r = 0.998). Based on the Chinese National Standard, this study provides a novel and powerful platform for the simple, rapid, sensitive and on-site analysis of 3-MCPD in soy sauce.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Paper-based sensor
Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs)
Carbon dot
3-Chloro-1,2-propandiol
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs with amino groups
Preparation and characterization of MIPs/CDs grafted paper
Adsorption and selectivity of MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based sensor
Detection of 3-MCPD by the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM micrographs of bare filter paper (A), CDs-paper (B) and MIPs/CDs grafted paper (C). Photographs of filter paper, CDs-paper and MIPs/CDs grafted paper under ultraviolet lamp.

Adsorption isotherms of 3-MCPD on the MIPs/CDs grafted paper and NIPs/CDs grafted paper, respectively. The diameter of paper was 8 cm, 10 mL of aqueous solution of ethanol (80%, v/v) was used as solvent, and the contact time was 2 h.

Rebinding kinetic behavior of 3-MCPD on the MIPs/CDs grafted paper and NIPs/CDs grafted paper, respectively. The concentration of 3-MCPD was 100 μg mL−1. Inset: the pseudo-second-order mode for the adsorption of 3-MCPD.

(A) The effect of different interfering compounds on the adsorption of 3-MCPD (100 μg mL−1) on the MIPs/CDs grafted paper and NIPs/CDs grafted paper, respectively. (B) Comparison of the fluorescence signal of sensor in response to the target 3-MCPD or interfering compounds. The concentration of interfering compounds was 500 μg mL−1.

Typical fluorescence curves for 3-MCPD based on the extraction and detection of MIPs/CDs grafted paper. The concentrations are 0, 1, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100, and 150 ng mL−1, respectively. Inset: the calibration curve of the proposed method.
A comparison of paper grafted sensor and GC–MS methods for 3-MCPD determination in soy sauce.

head.ce:title: A molecularly imprinted polymers/carbon dots-grafted paper sensor for 3-monochloropropane-1,2-diol determination

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Food Science and Engineering, Wuhan Polytechnic University, Wuhan 430023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Processing of Agricultural Products, Wuhan Polytechnic University, Wuhan 430023, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Processing of Agricultural Products

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Institute of Food Science and Engineering, Wuhan Polytechnic University, Wuhan 430023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 3-Monochloropropane-1,2-diol (3-MCPD) is a common food processing contaminant and a simple, rapid, sensitive and low cost monitoring technology is needed due to its potential carcinogenic nature. Carbon dots directly intercepted on filter paper provide high fluorescence intensity and can be adapted for use as a sensor. We synthesized a carbon dot-filter paper in combination with a molecularly imprinted polymeric film to extract 3-MCPD from samples. This grafted paper-based sensor exhibited a high adsorption capacity (68.97 mg g−1), an excellent selectivity (imprinting factor = 4.5) and a low detection limit (0.6 ng mL−1). Recoveries ranged from 97.2% to 105.3% with relative standard deviations <5.6%. The results obtained using this method were linearly correlated to those of the classic GC–MS method (r = 0.998). Based on the Chinese National Standard, this study provides a novel and powerful platform for the simple, rapid, sensitive and on-site analysis of 3-MCPD in soy sauce.

ce:section.ce:para: The 3-monochloropropane-1,2-diol (3-MCPD) is a by-product of acid hydrolysis of vegetable proteins and is a suspected human carcinogen (Jedrkiewicz, Kupska, Glowacz, Gromadzka & Namiesnik, 2016). 3-MCPD is present in both free and bound forms in numerous food products including soy sauces, meat products and infant foods (Lee & Khor, 2015). 3-MCPD is a group 2B carcinogen and the European Commission limits are 0.02 mg kg−1 in hydrolyzed vegetable protein and soy sauce. The maximum tolerable daily intake (TDI) was established at 2 µg kg−1 per day. In 2008, the Food and Drug Administration set a limit of 1 mg kg−1 in these products. In response to strict legislation limits, industry has made efforts to decrease the amount of 3-MCPD in hydrolyzed vegetable protein.

Gas chromatography with mass spectrometry (GC–MS) is used as a sensitive and classical approach for the detection of 3-MCPD (Kuesters et al., 2010; Kim et al., 2015; Yang, Kwon, Choi & Jo, 2018). However, due to the reactivity and polarity of 3-MCPD, the samples must be derivatized to improve peak shape. High performance liquid chromatography fluorescence detection coupled with derivatization was also developed for the determination of 3-MCPD (Hu, Cheng, Guo, Zhang & Qi, 2013). Subsequently, gas chromatography-triple quadruple mass spectrometry was proposed for the detection of 3-MCPD to circumvent derivatization (Genualdi, Nyman & DeJager, 2017). However, the chromatography methods still pose technical problems including low m/z fragmentation patterns, expensive instrumentation and high running costs. These methods are not suitable for high-throughput screening or on-site detection. The development of a rapid, cost effective and simple method was needed for the determination of 3-MCPD in food samples. The greatest challenge was to overcome matrix interference while retaining sensitivity.

Molecular imprinting is a promising strategy to decrease matrix interference and improve selective enhancement of target molecules (Ashley et al., 2017). Molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) can be matched to the size and shape of template molecules and exhibit excellent selectivity over structurally related compounds (Speltini, Scalabrini, Maraschi, Sturini & Profumo, 2017; Uzun & Turner, 2016; Yanez-Sedeno, Campuzano & Pingarron, 2017). An indirect electrochemical sensor has been developed for 3-MCPD detection based on the MIP/nano Au electrode using potassium ferrocyanide as a probe (Sun et al., 2014). However, many factors adversely affected the performance of this MIP sensor, such as electrode polishing and modification, temperature and humidity, anti-fouling performance, interference of active molecules and so on.

Paper-based devices provide new platforms for sensitive, high-throughput assays of food (Alamo Busa et al., 2016). The most attractive features of this technology are its low cost, ease of use and portability. The combination of the MIP technique with a paper-based device provides a promising strategy (Li et al., 2017; Qi et al., 2017). An anion-imprinted polymer grafted paper-based sensor for detection of Cd (II) ions via a color reaction between dithizone and cadmium has been developed (Huang et al., 2017). A direct competitive assay and labeled probes are necessary for this MIP-based sensor, but sensitivity is still a challenge. Thus, the right method for the future is one that blends a paper-based device and MIPs/fluorescence composites. Such a sensor would exhibit high selectivity for the target molecules and have low matrix interference but also possess the handling properties of a common paper device.

Imprinting using fluorescence monomers or encapsulating fluorescent nanoparticles have been applied to a MIPs-based fluorescence sensor (Chen, Wang, Lu, Wu & Li, 2016). However, the fluorescence monomers are relatively rare and the synthesis procedure is complex. Fluorescence materials and especially carbon dots (CD) are widely used for rapid detection sensors. They possess narrow emission spectra and have a strong signal intensity (Wang & Qiu, 2016; Li, Rui, Song, Shen & Zeng, 2015).

In this study, we used carbon dots possessing amino groups for attachment to filter paper containing carboxyl group susing negative pressure and heating. The MIP film was synthesized using 3-MCPD as a template and methacrylicacid (MAA) as a monomer. This was directly formed on the surface due to the amino group and carboxyl group interactions. This paper-based sensor was tested for its use as a selective and sensitive detection of 3-MCPD in soy sauce.

3-MCPD, 1,3-dichloro-2-propanol (1,3-DCP), 2,3-dichloro-1-propanol (2,3-DCP), glycerol, ethylene glycol and 1,2-propanediol were purchased from J&K Chemical. MAA, ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). MAA and EGDMA were purified by general distillation in vacuum under nitrogen to remove the polymerization inhibitor. AIBN was recrystallized from cooled methanol and dried at room temperature in a vacuum. Citric acid anhydrous, ethylenediamine, methanol, acetonitrile, acetone, ethanol and sodium hydroxide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Whatman 934-AH filter paper was purchased from Whatman International Ltd (Maidstone, England). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-R04 purification system (Millipore, Germany). Soy sauces were collected from a local supermarket and stored at -4 °C until analysis.

Fluorescent spectra was obtained using a Jasco FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, Japan) with excitation at 360 nm and emission from 380 to 600 nm for the CDs. FT-IR spectra were collected on a Verte 70 Fourier transform spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Scanning electron microscopic (SEM) images were observed using a Nova NanoSEM 450 microscope. The chemical composition of MIPs/CDs on the grafted paper was measured using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Axis-Ultra DLD 600 W, Japan) using Al/Mg Kα radiation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs with amino groups
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical procedure, 15 g citric acid anhydrous and 7.5 mL ethylenediamine were dissolved in 60 mL water and then sonicated for 10 min. The mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steelpan and heated at constant temperature of 200 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting solution was dialyzed against DI water for 48 h to remove impurities. The resulting mixture was freeze-dried.

The filter paper substrate was soaked in 5 M H2O2 for 3 h and washed with ultrapure water three times. CDs with amino groups flowed through the filter paper 10 times at 0.1 mL min−1 by using a negative pressure while heating at 80 °C. A one-pot synthesis strategy was then used to construct a MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based sensor based on the electrostatic attraction between amino groups (CDs) and carboxyl groups (MAA). Briefly, 2 mM MAA, 20 mM EGDMA, 1 mM 3-MCPD and the CDs intercepted filter paper (2 cm × 4 cm) were added to 10 mL aqueous solution of ethanol (80%, v/v). The resulting solution was shaken for 30 min for pre-polymerization. After purging with N2 for 10 min, 20 mg of AIBN was added, and the mixture was shaken at 60 °C overnight in the dark. The resulting products (MIPs/CDs grafted paper) were washed with acetonitrile/acetic acid (9/1, v/v) and then methanol to remove the templates and unreacted components and solvent, respectively. As a control, the non-imprinted composite (NIPs/CDs grafted paper) were prepared using an identical procedure without the addition of 3-MCPD.

MIPs/CDs grafted paper was further cut using a hole-puncher (8 mm × 8 mm) to avoid the boundary effect and incubated in 1 mL ethanol (80%, v/v) with 3-MCPD levels ranging from 10 to 150 μg mL−1. The filter paper was shaken for 24 h at room temperature and recovered using tweezers. The compounds in the supernatant were measured using a GC–MS method. The Freundlich and Langmuir isotherms were applied to study the adsorption mechanism of MIPs/CDs grafted paper. The curves of binding versus time were plotted to determine the adsorption kinetics for the target molecules.

Soy sauce samples were provided by a local supermarket and simultaneously analyzed by the classical GC–MS method and the proposed method. After spiking with different concentrations of 3-MCPD, the samples were ultrasonically vibrated for 10 min and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min to remove sediment of samples. The resulting solution was extracted with 6 mL of dichloromethane three times for 10 min each. After drying under a nitrogen flow at room temperature, the residue was dissolved in 1 mL of ethanol/0.01 M PBS buffer (pH = 7.0, 1/1, v/v). The extraction solution was slowly passed through the MIPs/CDs grafted paper at 0.1 mL min−1, using negative pressure and heat (50 °C). The paper was washed with 2 mL water to remove the sample matrix and placed in a 96-well plate for fluorescence detections. There was no need to elute 3-MCPD from the MIP composites and this improved sensitivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of MIPs/CDs grafted paper
ce:section.ce:para: Filter paper is a base material that provides numerous hydroxyl groups and a 3-dimensional support for the preparation of multifunction polymers. We activated OH groups by incubation in H2O2 and CDs with amino groups were prepared by a hydrothermal method for use a fluorescence probe. The average of CDs was approximately 4.7 nm with a positive zeta potential (18.9 mV), indicating the presence of positively charged groups (Fig. S1, Supplementary material). The CDs were introduced into the filter paper using negative pressure and heat, and they attached via electrostatic interactions. A similar procedure was used to modify isopropenyl groups on the CDs-paper, utilizing the amino groups (CDs) and carboxylgroups (MAA). A simple and easy free radical polymerization enabled MIPs/CDs grafted paper using 3-MCPD as a template and MAA as a functional monomer. MAA played an important role in the imprinting polymerization, not only as a proton donor (H12) but also as a proton acceptor (O10). It interacted with the template through formation of strong hydrogen bonds as 3-MCPD has H-bond acceptors. The 3-MCPD and MAA complex is a low energy state occurring via two hydrogen-bonding sites; a carbonyl and a carboxyl (Li et al., 2014). After removing the template, the resulting composites could be applied for selective recognition and sensitive detection of 3-MCPD. Without the sol-gel film modification on the CDs, MIPs/CDs grafted paper exhibit high fluorescence intensity and sensitivity. This sensor still presents superior features such as low cost, simple measurement, high throughput, and facile portability and operation.

To study the modified morphologic structures, images of bare filter paper, CDs-paper and MIPs/CDs grafted paper were observed using SEM. The filter paper showed a non-uniform three-dimensional network structure (Fig. 1 A). After negative pressure and heat, the CDs were evenly and tightly inserted into the filter paper and are necessary for the reproducibility of fluorescence measurements (Fig. 1B). After polymerization, a uniform MIPs film was grafted onto the CDs surface and the fibers were coated (Fig. 1C). After MIPs polymerization, the imprinted paper still possessed high fluorescence intensity and was only slightly weaker than that of the CD-paper (Fig. 1D). This result demonstrated a novel strategy for the CDs-MIPs interactions without chemical crosslinking.

The FT-IR spectra showed strong absorption peaks at 1091 cm−1 and wide Si-O-Si symmetric stretching for the glass fiber support (Whatman filter paper). The absorption peak at 1637 cm−1 was the result of residual vinyl C = C bond stretching (Fig. S2, Supplementary material) (Oliveira, Machado, Barth & Acosta, 2016). The results revealed that the MIPs were grafted successfully on the CDs-filter paper. We also used X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy to investigate the composition of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper. The C1s spectral peak centered at 284.6 eV was appropriate for C = C groups and for MIPs using free radical polymerization. The N atom concentration was increased from 0.03% to 1.87% after coating. Furthermore, the N1s band could be de-convoluted into three components at 401.8 (-C-N-), 400.6 (–NH-C = O) and 399.8 eV (–NH2) (Bin Yousaf et al., 2017). The 400.6 and 399.8 eV peaks were indicative of the CDs and the presence of electrostatic attractions between the filter paper-CDs and MAA (Fig. S3, Supplementary material).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and selectivity of MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The adsorption capacity and selectivity are important factors for the proper operation of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper sensor. A high adsorption capacity is beneficial for recovery and its use as an analytical technique. Greater selectivity acts to decrease sample matrix interference and improve precision (Hao et al., 2016; Zhang, Cui, Cai, Duan & Liu, 2015).

We found that the static adsorption capacities of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper and the NIPs/CDs grafted paper were 64.3 and 20.2 mg g−1, respectively (Fig. 2 ). The imprinting factor was calculated as 3.17 based on the ratio of the adsorption capacities of MIPs and NIPs. As a supporting material, the adsorption capacity of 3-MCPD on the CDs-filter paper was only 0.02 mg g−1, indicating a highly selective adsorption ability of the MIPs film. Furthermore, the Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms of MIPs and NIPs indicated that 3-MCPD absorption onto the MIP film followed a Langmuir isotherm model and that all imprinted sites had equal affinities for the target molecules. The maximum adsorption capacity of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper for 3-MCPD was 68.97 mg g−1 (Table S1, Supplementary material). Previous studies had reported a MIPs sensor that used 3-MCPD as a template possessed a maximum adsorption capacity of 6.39 mg g−1 and 2.20 mg g−1 for NIPs (Li et al., 2014). Another study synthesized a MIPs-based chemical sensor with a maximum adsorption capacity for 3-MCPD of 11.65 mg g−1 (Leung, Chiu & Lam, 2003). Our MIPs/CDs grafted paper sensor exhibited high adsorption ability and excellent selectivity for 3-MCPD due to the surface imprinting technology and introduction of different types of functional groups.

We also carried out dynamic adsorption tests that indicated the equilibrium time of 3-MCPD on the MIPs/CDs grafted paper was 30 min (Fig. 3 ). This was most likely the result of the large surface area and the surface imprinting strategy. Furthermore, pseudo-first-order, pseudo-second-order and intra-particle diffusion models were applied to study the mechanism and rate-controlling step in the extraction of 3-MCPD. The adsorption kinetic constants and the regression coefficients indicated that the R2 value for pseudo-second-order kinetics was the greatest. The calculated values of the adsorption capacity (Qe, calc) were almost identical to the experimental values (Qe, exp) (Table S1, Supplementary material).

Selective recognition plays an important role in the control of sample matrix interference and trace detection for 3-MCPD. We studied these process by comparing the binding ability of the template molecule with potential interfering compounds ethylene glycol, glycerol, 1,2-propanediol, 1,3-DCP and 2,3-DCP. The introduction of these chemicals did not interfere with the selective adsorption of 3-MCPD (Fig. 4 A). The quenching effects of the fluorescence response could be modeled using the Stem-Volmer equation.
(1)
(
F 0
/ F
)
- 1 =
K
SV
× C
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively; KSV is the quenching constant and C is the concentration of 3-MCPD. The ratio of Ksv, MIPs to Ksv, NIPs was defined as the imprinting factor (IF) equaled 4.5, indicating a highly selective recognition. There was a loss of only 1.7–10.6% of the fluorescence intensity caused by the added chemicals and these were most likely due to the presence of imprinted cavities (Fig. 4B). Furthermore, there was almost no fluorescence quenching of MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based towards real sample blanks (RSD < 6%). This indicated only 3-MCPD was efficiently recognized and this recognition resulted in significant changes in the fluorescence intensity. Most importantly, the MIPs film could block the interfering compounds, protected the CDs fluorescence and improved its practical application.

3-MCPD detection is a combination of surface adsorption and electronic transfer that can result in fluorescence quenching. In the absence of the correct orientation of template molecule in the imprinted cavities, CD electrons can receive UV energy and fluoresce during their return to the ground state. An increase in template concentration leads to decreased CDs energy levels and is the analytical principle of the MIPs/CD grafted paper-based sensor. Therefore, we optimized the size of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper by altering the solution pH and extraction time to generate a low detection limit with a wide linear range.

The size of the MIPs/CDs grafted onto the paper determined its mass and extraction ability. The minimum amount of paper-based sensor required was 8 × 8 mm and enabled complete recovery at 93.5 ± 2.7%. Increasing the paper size did not produce any significant improvement in recovery. This would however increase the possibility of sample matrix interference. Ethanol was used as an extraction solution to increase the solubility of 3-MCPD and ensure the selective recognition ability of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper. The water content of the extraction solvent plays an important role in H-bonding between 3-MCPD and MAA (Zhang et al., 2015; Fan et al., 2016). Moreover, polar solvents and particularly water, can provide hydrophobic interactions that are nonspecific but can increase adsorption. Thus, PBS buffer was beneficial for the selective extraction and further deceased matrix interference and ethanol/0.01 M PBS buffer (pH = 7.0, 1/1, v/v) was the optimal solvent (Fig. S4, Supplementary material). Further increases of PBS buffer in the mixture would disturb the interactions based on H-bonding (Shen, Zhang & Ren, 2015). Thus, the selective mechanism here might be more complicated and both of hydrogen bonding and the hydrophobic forces played important roles between 3-MCPD and MIPs. We further studied effects of buffer pH between 5.0 and 8.5. The fluorescence intensity was maximal at pH 7.0 and decreased significantly at lower and higher pH values. At pH > 6.0, functional carboxyl groups were deprotinated enabling the MAA -3-MCPD interaction (Jafari, Nasirizadeh & Dehghani, 2017). However, under basic conditions both MIPs and 3-MCPD were deprotonated and the fluorescence intensity was decreased. Thus, ethanol/0.01 M PBS buffer (pH = 7.0, 1/1, v/v) was used to selectively extract 3-MCPD in further experiments.

Extraction time also played a role in fluorescence intensity of the sensor, 10 min was sufficient to achieve complete recovery, and the fluorescence intensity reached a plateau during this period (Fig. S5B, Supplementary material). Short extraction times are beneficial for on-site food safety analyses. Therefore, 10 min was fixed as the optimized extraction time used for further experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of 3-MCPD by the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practical applications of our method, we used it to determine 3-MCPD levels in soy sauce samples. The fluorescence intensity (F0/F-1) was dose dependent and linear from 1 to 150 ng mL−1with an R2 of 0.9991 (Fig. 5 ), changes in fluorescence intensity were still observed down to 0.6 ng mL−1 but this was off scale and could not be quantitated. Thus, the limit of detection (LOD) of the proposed method was 0.6 ng mL−1. The European Commission (EC) set a limit of 20 μg kg−1 for 3-MCPD in soy sauce and the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 2717–2003) is 5 μg kg−1. Therefore, this sensor meets both these limits for 3-MCPD detection in soy sauce.

The fluorescence stability of the grafted sensor was tested during at different storage times in the dark at room temperature. We found no significant change in fluorescence intensity of the MIPs/CDs grafted paper after 12 measurements within 60 min (RSD < 5.2%). The sensor displayed good physical stability and chemical inertness. Most importantly, the reproducibility of the paper-based sensor was examined using a series of MIP composites. The RSD values were <9.4% (n = 5) and this was attributed to the uniform CD distribution on the filter paper and the surface MIP polymerization. Although the device has weak mechanical stability due to its paper construction, the low cost makes it an ideal disposable device for 3-MCPD detection.

In this study, we simultaneously analyzed real samples and compared our new method with the classical GC–MS method (GB/T 2717–2003) to validate performance. The spiked concentrations of 3-MCPD were 1 ng mL−1, 5 ng mL−1, 10 ng mL−1and 50 ng mL−1, respectively. Recoveries from spiked samples ranged from 97.2 to 105.3% and RSD levels from 2.8% to 5.6%. Precision was analyzed by assaying spiked samples over five days and resulted in RSD values of 4.5 to 8.3%. Moreover, the correlation between the results of the two methods indicated that the new method was reliable with an R-value of 0.998 and a relative error <6.34% (Table 1 ). This indicated that our new sensor technology is a reliable technique for 3-MCPD determinations in real samples.

In this study, a MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based sensor was prepared and then developed for the detection of 3-MCPD. Based on the electrostatic attraction, CDs were directly imprinted into the filter paper to provide high fluorescence intensity and a MIP film was synthesized on the CDs-paper to selectively extract 3-MCPD. The maximum adsorption capacity was 68.97 mg g−1 and the pseudo second-order model was valid for the selective adsorption of 3-MCPD. Under optimized conditions, the linear range was 1–150 ng mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.6 ng mL−1. Based on the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 2717-2003), MIPs/CDs grafted paper-based sensor can be used for the detection of 3-MCPD in soy sauce. Furthermore, this approach offers many attractive advantages such as high sensitivity, rapid response, easy operation, as well as low cost and portability. The proposed sensor can also be developed for detection of other target molecules by designing new MIP films.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hubei Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2016CFB119) and The National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFC1600500).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2018.08.133.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
